item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't like it at all. The author's approach is very non-inventive, he simply makes depression an abnormal epidemic disease and list its symptoms in the form of a Foundation protocol.

Infinitely big doubts are caused by the structure of the narrative, where the character somehow writes about his thoughts in the form of a report on the anomaly, which is rather bizarre and unnatural. Meta for the sake of meta. The blue letters are a pretty typical case of Internet whining.

The only remotely funny thing is the provided distinction between depression and melancholy and some hint that an epidemic of undiagnosed depression is raging in the world.

Neither an interesting interpretation of the phenomenon of depression, nor a good literary text. It looks like sublimation of the author's, a therapeutic text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think offset 4 is missing a blue U to form Claudia
?
Congratularations aye haych pee you made a good thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too real. Neutral voting for actually getting the creeps, that's a first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is an explanation for depression i guess.

item.title: Anomalous?
item.content:encoded:
What makes this an SCP and not, say, just a regular old explainable disease?

The closeness to reality might have betrayed it, despite the fact it's a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One Nitpick:
In the first iteration it says "And you cry."
I feel like that should be in blue in the next iteration, not in the first one.

Other than that +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Hits hard and close to home for me. The voice was very relatable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah fuck. I'm crying again. I've had major depression for most of my life, and… This hit hard. +1

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I was just going to modify the first fragment. As for the message, do you happen to have any ideas to change it, if you don't like it?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I'm no neurologist, but 'dulling the neurons' sounds like technobabble to me. Also, 940 affects the whole body, hence their name being "Araneae Marionettes"; while 3020 manifests in various parts of the body, it mainly affects brain chemistry.

One last point: this article uses the ListPages module, and I'm confused as to on which of the fragment pages this would go; on all pages? On just the first or second one?

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: vetoed by author in chat

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

I believe that this could be linked to SCP-940. They are both parasites that mess with the brain in some way, either by interfacing with the nervous system or eating their dopamine.

Editing this:

SCP-3020 is small enough to pass the blood-brain barrier, and manifests solely in human beings, who are also the sole vector of transmission of SCP-3020.

To this:

SCP-3020 is small enough to pass the blood-brain barrier1, and manifests solely in human beings, who are also the sole vector of transmission of SCP-3020.

Footnotes
1. At this point, SCP-3020 also dulls neurons in the brain to prevent detection. It is of note that it interfaces with the nervous system in a similar manner to SCP-940. A link between the two anomalies has yet to be established.
IMO, it lacks enough anomaly to be an SCP, and is more like describing Depression and some feelings about it. It lacks the "unspeakable horror" too..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guess I have an SCP-3020 infection. Lock me up, boys.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon first reading, I had a fear this would come out quite bland. Mental Illness had been explored in a good number of the other SCPs that I'd read, so it felt like it might turn out to be "one of the other ones". But the more I read, the more you hooked me, especially with the leak on the first page that was a personal comment.

I've had Depression and Anxiety most of my life, and this is an extremely emotional read for me. You've captured the darkest part of being afraid of your mental health in a SCP about a parasite: The fear of "not making it". The fear that all the pain will lead up to nothing and nowhere. This entire piece felt like an extended metaphor, and I love metaphors. It's almost poetic, really, the way this has been written and presented. +1 from me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that he's suggesting that SCP-999 and SCP-500 might like "counter" SCP-3020, since one makes you feel euphoric, and one cures you of all ailments. No idea bout the 343 part though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about them? What do they have to do with this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP 999, SCP 343, and SCP 500?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was the inclusion of some of the author's second-person "personal notes" in the main article intentional? The "and you cry" paragraph feels like it leaked in from the subsequent offsets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. I especially like the sort of "unofficial edits" by the author that detail the dark descent into self loathing that this thing causes. It is…. relatable. Way. Too. Damn. relatable. And thats why you get an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ouch. Hit a little bit too close. Upvoting.

item.title: Shit.
item.content:encoded:
Nothing related to the actual piece.

But a great friend of mine is named Claudia…so that just made this sting a tiny bit more.

Now actually related to this…+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nitpicky, but high dopamine is generally believed to cause anger (there's some newer stuff saying anger may have a connection to low dopamine, but mostly high dopamine = anger)

item.title: In Response
item.content:encoded:
I am really not cool with having SCPs that explain away real world phenomena.

Half of the comments on here are like this, so I figure it's time to address this.

This is not meant to explain an actual, dangerous medical condition as an SCP. This is meant to be a contagious anomaly that causes symptoms similar to clinical depression that cannot be treated by traditional means due to the destruction of dopamine for the sake of survival.

This is not meant to be exploitative. I've addressed that already in a reply to GeneR's comment on page 2.

This was formed out of personal fears of my own: that my treatment for depression isn't working, and that my depression is going to harm those I love in some way, metaphorically infect them. Why do I deserve to be scrutinized for putting my own mental issues and fears out there on the table when so many people on the wiki do the same in their writing?

This is meant to attempt to show depression through the lens of an SCP. It's impossible to do it objectively without seeming like a robot, and it's impossible to do it subjectively without getting criticism saying that it's explaining away a real condition as an anomaly. I tried to do a mixture of both, and I'm wondering if it would have been better to do neither.

That's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I uh, I'm sure somebody else has said this, but like, I am really not cool with having SCPs that explain away real world phenomena. There was an SCP that was talking about how… something sentient caused obesity in the US, and I didn't care for that at all either. Yes, yes, you nailed depression. Yes, that you did. I think. I mean, I've been there, but… ah fuck it, I'm not going into personal details right now. Yeah, you got a lot of the things down pat.

But something in-universe saying "depression isn't a real human thing, it's a parasite" is… well, symbolic for one thing, but unreasonable. I am downvoting based on sentiment. Otherwise, it was a pretty well written article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am teetering, and I like to read comments when I'm teetering.
I definitely felt frustrated and annoyed that they wouldn't let the guy who clearly wanted to die kill himself, but that was mostly just annoyance and not fear, as I'm sure many depressed people have felt. I have to agree with the person who said this is mostly just an empathy article. However, I had never even thought about the point of having depression (and/or Parkinson's) that's infectious until seeing the comment above. That's horrifying, and it was almost enough to get my +1…but there's too much of this that isn't.

I definitely don't like the last bit. Living with depression I don't feel is the worse outcome of all of that, frankly. I'm not sure that suicide might have been the best call (for my personal enjoyment; I'm not going to speak for others) either, though. Maybe dying to protect someone else or something like that?
Either way, this article comes off like 'regular' clinical depression is the worst thing that can ever happen, when plenty of worse, much more horrific things happen to the characters before they even get to stage 6. AKA 'regular' depression.
In fact, I'm shocked at how much the tremors are completely glossed over as just being manageable.
I also have to agree that the format reads too much like a diary and not enough like an article for the way it's framed. I'd love it to just be a journal entry instead, one with a little more background into things like the Parkinson's and maybe even the reasons the foundation were *so* against these two offing themselves.
All I really felt when I read this was "This SCP is what it's like to be in an asylum after an attempt. I feel a great amount of empathy, but it's not scary. It's not even really an SCP."
And actually, not being an SCP might be a big enough issue for me to slide this into negative…
I feel really strongly about a viral form of what is by far the worst aspect of myself, but there are too many things wrong with this for me to stay neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "official" iteration of the SCP has bits and pieces of Lawrence's personal edits in it, and while I can see it being a part of the format screw I feel like the "official" version shouldn't have any nonclinical tone in it. That is really my only technical criticism.

I'm gonna be honest, this didn't scare me in the slightest. I've dealt with depression for seven or so years now, and while the symptoms here align with the issues I had (minus the Parkinson's symptoms), I don't find this SCP all that frightening. Granted, that's very much in the eye of the beholder because being depressed forever and ever is probably a very real fear for some people with depression, but to me this feels like I'm looking at my own thought processes. I certainly sympathized with Lawrence and Claudia, but nothing here really scared me.

I do have an opinion on the topic of making SCPs out of actual disorders and diseases (that opinion being "we probably shouldn't do that") that tends to affect my voting choice, but given that you're writing this from first-hand experience I'm far more inclined to not be too critical on that matter.

Neutral vote, with noted appreciation for no "and then they killed themselves" type ending

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that depression is horrible and stuff, and that writing about it is something that happens alot, and if you have deppression I understand from a second hand experience, but I don't see how this is an SCP really. It's more like a page on MedMD, the article isn't really on anomalous anything, or relating to the anomaly or SCP besides the format of the webpage. It's a really cool idea and all, but I don't see this fitting as an SCP at all, the disease isn't anomalous whatsoever, it's a micro-organism that feeds off of the hosts dopamine. It's more of a depressed person describing their life experiences. There's also to much heart and personality put into it for an SCP article IMO, it's more like you're writing a book honestly, it's not professional enough. This could work pretty well as something in the horror genre, and it defenitely speaks to alot of people, but I don't think it fit's in as an SCP article at all. For that, I'm either downvoting or abstaining from voting, sorry about that. You can convert it into a story to put in the archived section or the short stories section, but it really doesn't seem like it should be catagorized as a numbered SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's weird that even the first iteration had the informal "So, you do. You bury yourself under work," even though this was supposed to be written before Dr. Lawrence Packard's exposure to SCP-3020.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This all feels underwhelming. I'm sure the whole depression breakdown thing would have been more effective if I didn't read 3999/LordStonefish's entry and the multiple iterations thing feels like it was underutilized. At least it ends with a sort of bittersweet ending.

item.title: Re: I'm no biologist, but...
item.content:encoded: Oh, right, I didn't realize Dopamine can't cross the Blood-brain barrier. Still, counseling as part of treatment probably wouldn't hurt.

item.title: Re: I'm no biologist, but...
item.content:encoded: The organism doesn't just live in the kidneys. The reason that they give the injections there is that there are two places in the body you want to be really sure get L-DOPA, and it's harder to give injections to the brain.

item.title: I'm no biologist, but...
item.content:encoded:
This thing eats L-DOPA. So. Why are we injecting its victims with more L-DOPA, when we could just bypass the kidneys entirely and inject the patients with finished Dopamine administered via intravenous drip?

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dopamine_(medication)

This would improve the patients' moods without actively feeding the organism, and may even avert the loss of treatment effectiveness towards the end of Stage 3. This, in turn, would reduce suicidal tendencies and may lead to better long-term outcomes, with more patients surviving Stage 5 and better overall quality-of-life for survivors.

Additionally, the combination of medication and therapy is often more effective than medication alone in treating mundane depression IRL, so it might make sense to recommend therapy alongside whatever chemical treatment the containment procedures recommend. It may be a good idea to compare and contrast with the closest possible analogue from DSM-V, to the extent that symptoms and treatments differ.

The next logical step would be to suppress the Kidneys' production of L-DOPA in patients, so as to starve the organism of its food source. While it can apparently exist indefinitely outside the body, without an energy source, at least it won't be reproducing.

item.title: SCP-3020: Depression
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't expecting this to get into the top twenty; I honestly thought this would flounder around +50, at most, especially since it seems to be rather controversial in subject material.

I'd just like to say this: I regret nothing about the presentation here. This is how I view depression, and how I fear it. If you don't view it that way, then fair enough. Everyone's mind works differently. I'm not trying to present this as literally the only way depression works. Hell, this isn't even the only way 3020 works. A large majority of this is from the perspective of a very troubled researcher as he tries to cope with his life spiraling out of control and his condition affecting those around him. Both this and actual depression are diseases, and everyone's body reacts differently to disease

I have a lot more I could say with regards to some of the feedback I got on this after posting, but I'll just leave it at this: I'm glad it at least got to 15th. Thanks to everyone who did- and hopefully will- upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3000 is a microscopic organism of unknown genera and species, measuring less than a micron in length.

About 1/5 th of a micron if the similarity to Rabies lyssavirus is meant descriptively. Which makes "less than a micron" pretty imprecise.

SCP-3000 was believed to be most closely related to the Rabies lyssavirus virus, but observation has led the Foundation to believe that SCP-3000 is actually a parasitic organism resembling a viral body.

This struck me as vague to meaninglessness. Rabies lyssavirus itself is a "parasitic organism resembling a viral body" as long as you don't split meaningless hairs about "organisms".

Until recently, the identity SCP-3000 was ambiguous, but recent testing has showed that SCP-3000 is indeed a member of the kingdom Animalia and not a virus.

This is, I think, the only actual anomaly here. Really it's hard for me to even imagine how this is possible. It simply can't be an animal. It is smaller than the smallest possible cells and animals are by definition multicellular. I am afraid this is simply nonsense.

As an anomaly it is too thin, IMO, to hang an SCP article on. This really strikes me as something that the Foundation would make -EX(plained) and then distribute into conventional medical publications.

I think that I am reluctantly downvoting, despite the emotional impact this has and the otherwise high quality of the writing.

item.title: Re: This Author Commentary Is Hype For 3000
item.content:encoded:
Pages have to have a fragment: in front of their name.

This isn't true. SCP-2111 doesn't use categories at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid I don't listen to SCPReadings, or really follow any SCP Foundation fan videos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I somehow got BSL-1 and -4 mixed up. Thankfully I still have a day to edit it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, but I gotta ask, SCPReadings, aka Gemodawn made a video called "Depression should be an SCP" so, did you get the idea from her? Anyways, great entry.

item.title: Typo
item.content:encoded: Is the typo in iteration 2 ("Who hut Claudia?") intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My impression was that by the end the sickness had run its course and any feeling of depression was likely due to them being locked up and isolated.

I was really expecting something neat when it was mentioned that it took the form of 5 stages and was expecting a neat deconstruction or inversion of the 5 stages of grieving but if there was an attempt at that then I didn't see it certainly stage 3 and 5 are in the grieving process but it seemed like a coincidence based on the 5 stages of 3000.

As someone who has depression I felt insulted in the middle and had to force myself to finish it expecting some kind of payoff that would justify it, but I found no payoff. I have to agree with Gene R's use of both exploitative and sympathy. Regardless of who created it and their intent I felt like it was an attempt at exploiting and melodramatizing depression. Maybe its because I have bi-polar depression instead of regular depression, who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The horror will definitely be lost on certain people, especially those without depression (or a similar disorder).

Although, I feel that this is VERY over direct in terms of it's story, saying that "you just have to live with depression", is just stupidly cheesy and unoriginal imo.

This kind of sob story belongs on some rando's tumblr blog, not the foundation archives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some good lines in this, but some of it reads as pretty cheesy. Couple that with it getting really repetitive at times, and I found it hard to really draw myself into the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I probably should elaborate.

As I read this article, I had several things on my mind. First, iterations are a bandwagon people jump on all too gladly. Second, onset and symptoms of depression are shown pretty well here. Third, the article is essentially a wrap for a tale about depression.

Not that any of these is something bad per se.

The feelings I had while reading this article were along the lines of "Yeah, I had something similar, I can relate to that". It didn't evoke any fear regarding the things depicted here. Instead, the article evoked sympathy. I had the feeling this was done intentionally, as depression is something quite widespread, moreso with this site's creative crowd.

If that wasn't done intentionally, this article was intended to scare, not to make people sympathize, and it's just the way I perceive it - fine, my point about "exploitation" rests. Still, I keep my downvote, because it just doesn't work as a horror story for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Random nitpick; shouldn't the containment procedures be BSL-4?

Overall I liked this. I can see why some criticize the way that the doctor's additions were actually made in a copy of the file, and I wasn't sure at first how I felt about the depression being a compulsion effect of sorts, but the reactions of the characters and the ending made it work well for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
masturbatory

You're still not over that? It was so small, so slight, so inane

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was a far more rational response than I think the comment deserved.

I mean, "at some point in their lives"? Yeah, Gene R can shut the hell up, and let's be thankful that this is a well-moderated forum and I'm ​not allowed to fully explain what I think of him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Living with it isn't horrible. It sucks, but you make do.

The horrible part is that you're infectious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredibly done. This is, without hesitation, the scariest SCP I've ever read. It hits my personal phobias intensely. It played with me all while I was reading it, which I like. And the ending… I'm not sure whether that's the scariest part or the bright spot in the whole thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna have to be in the minority on disliking this, I suppose. When I started reading this, I was not expecting that this would turn into a format-screwy narrative with first-person additions, and that first step into lack of clinical tone was jarring in a bad way. This article would have the same emotional weight just in terms of subject matter and content without involving two people's specific situation. I imagine that if this were written in the standard through-and-through clinical format, it would actually be more powerful, and would have freaked me out way more, from the standpoint of having MDD or the standpoint of just being a reader in general. I liked where this was going as a concept until it turned into one guy using an SCP article as a diary, basically (even if I can tell that the author took care in making his additions mostly focused on the skip, which is an aspect I picked up on and wouldn't want to overlook). Downvote for these reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang.

That final iteration is pretty chilling. I was expecting the good doctor to off himself, but having him and his wife together, forever stuck with such a terrible infection, was quite the powerful punch. I want to call the ending bittersweet (not something you see often in the horror contest), but that's just me.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i think on the onset 4 u wanted to put the letters C L A U D I A in blue from the description. but then i think u missed out a U.

edit: just finished reading this. my past can relate to this so much. flashbacks of memories are haunting me right now.
thanks for a trip down to memory lane, especially since i once knew someone called Claudia.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To a degree (and I get that this is a personal skip) there are flaws here: I feel the symptoms are an overgeneralization and that maybe one or two pairs of changelongs could have been merged to keep things streamlined. But the ending is just… I'm not sure if "poignant" is the right term, but it sealed this into upvote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made a promise to myself that I wouldn't get too involved with critique in this contest, and that I'd just let most of this slide, as I have a habit of getting overly-emotional over contests. If people don't find it scary, that's fine. It's a very subjective fear, and one that I've put up with for years. I've had stuff I've posted here called everything from masturbatory to unfunny to cartoonish. But "exploitative" is a new one, and one that I take issue with. Great issue, in fact.

I'm going to try to put this as delicately as I can. I've had depression for at least a decade, and my symptoms started getting particularly horrible when I was fifteen. Since then, I've made friends who have what I do, or worse, hanging over them. I can't speak for anyone else's experience, but to me, I dread what my depression will cause me to do- or not do- on any given day. Will I just give up and not go to class? Will I have a nervous breakdown? Will something happen that just causes me to go off the deep end entirely and lead to my life crumbling down?

This does not aim to exploit any more than any of the other entries do. This argument going to sound weaker than wet paper, but this is a contest about fear. In order to cause fear, you have to exploit the underlying causes. This is as targeted as people with depression as much as (sorry for using you as an example here, SpectralDragon) Abdominal Pain is targeted at people with Chron's disease. This is just aimed at anyone who has the fear that the worst day in your life is going to become the only day in your life- a fear that not many people on the site seem to have, but it's one of the only things that truly scares me.

That's all I have to say on the matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read it through, Well-written as it may be, it seemed more like an attempt to exploit the depression theme among those who had it at some point in their lives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very powerful piece of writing. I love it, I relate to it (learning to live with depression is something I'm still working on) and it tickles my writing fancies.

Shame that it isn't really scary.

I'll +1, but out of my respect for the content more then anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Living with depression/despair doesn't seem horrific to me.

I mean, I don't think my life is particularly horrific to me and I've learned to live with my demons. I don't feel suffering from them. I find it strange that the idea of living with your darknesses is scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good. It's really good. But it doesn't quite fit the contest theme. No vote until the contest is decided.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My one nitpick:
You forgot about the inflammation.1

Footnotes
1. he typed before opening the large bottle of aspirin on his desk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crap, now that I reached the final iteration, I don't know whether to upvote due to how relatable the horror is, or to leave my vote neutral because I now have a sense of relief. Still, the sense of depressing horror was definitely real when I went through the edits, so I guess I'll just give it a +1.

Still, if the author himself/herself is going through the same problem (which is usually the case for stuff like this), then I'd just like to remind them and everyone else going through depression that nothing lasts forever, both the good things and bad things. Stay strong!
